title: WAL recovery: replay durable WAL from last committed root to reconstruct buffer state
purpose: Deliver the crash-recovery complement to the durable WAL writer introduced in CORE-005. ADR-003 mandates that uncommitted mutations buffered in memory at crash time are recoverable from the on-disk WAL — this brief implements that recovery path. WalRecovery reads the durable WAL file written by DurableWal, validates each entry's CRC32 checksum, and replays mutations from the last committed root hash position forward into a fresh WalBuffer. The result is a reconstructed buffer indistinguishable from the one that existed before the crash, ready for the shard actor (CORE-007) to resume normal operation. Without this brief, the durable WAL file is written but never read on restart — crash safety is a guarantee only if recovery exists.
task: Create crates/haematite/src/wal/recovery.rs implementing WalRecovery. The recovery reader opens the durable WAL file, scans entries sequentially, and identifies the position of the last committed root hash (written by DurableWal as a Commit entry). It then replays all subsequent Put and Delete entries into a new WalBuffer, stopping at the first truncated entry (incomplete write from crash) or the end of file. Truncated entries are silently skipped — a truncated tail is the normal crash signature and is not an error. Corrupted entries (entry is complete but CRC32 does not match) are hard errors — corruption is distinct from truncation and indicates data integrity failure. After replay, the returned WalBuffer contains exactly the uncommitted mutations from the pre-crash shard state. Wire WalRecovery into wal/mod.rs. This brief does not implement the shard actor that calls recovery on restart — that is CORE-007.

requirements.title: WalRecovery type and open constructor
requirements.spec: Define a WalRecovery struct that holds an open file handle to the durable WAL. WHEN WalRecovery::open(path) is called, THE SYSTEM SHALL open the file at path for reading and return Ok(WalRecovery) if the file exists. IF the file does not exist, THE SYSTEM SHALL return WalError::Io wrapping the underlying io::Error. THE SYSTEM SHALL NOT truncate or modify the file on open — the file is read-only during recovery. WalRecovery SHALL implement Debug.
acceptance: WalRecovery::open(path) returns Ok when the file exists
WalRecovery::open(path) returns Err(WalError::Io(_)) when the file does not exist
Opening a WAL file does not modify or truncate it (file length is unchanged after open)
WalRecovery implements Debug

requirements.title: WAL frame parsing with CRC32 validation
requirements.spec: WHEN reading a frame from the WAL file, THE SYSTEM SHALL read the 4-byte little-endian payload length, then the payload bytes, then the 4-byte CRC32 checksum, matching the exact frame format written by DurableWal. THE SYSTEM SHALL verify the CRC32 of the payload bytes against the stored checksum using crc32fast::hash. IF the checksum does not match, THE SYSTEM SHALL return WalError::ChecksumMismatch { expected, actual } — this is a hard error indicating data corruption. IF the file ends mid-frame (fewer bytes than the length prefix or payload declares), THE SYSTEM SHALL treat the entry as truncated and return a sentinel indicating truncation, not an error. THE SYSTEM SHALL NOT silently ignore a complete frame whose CRC32 fails — truncation and corruption are distinct conditions.
acceptance: A well-formed frame (length + payload + matching CRC32) is parsed and returned as Ok(Some(payload_bytes))
A frame whose CRC32 does not match the payload returns Err(WalError::ChecksumMismatch { expected, actual })
Reading a frame when the file ends before the 4-byte length prefix returns Ok(None) indicating clean end-of-file
Reading a frame where the file ends after the length prefix but before all payload bytes returns Ok(None) indicating truncation
Reading a frame where the file ends after the payload but before the 4-byte checksum returns Ok(None) indicating truncation
A ChecksumMismatch error names both the expected (stored) and actual (computed) values

requirements.title: Mutation deserialisation matching DurableWal format
requirements.spec: WHEN a payload is decoded from a WAL frame, THE SYSTEM SHALL deserialise it using the same tag-prefixed format written by DurableWal: tag byte 0x01 for Mutation::Put (followed by 4-byte LE key length, key bytes, 4-byte LE value length, value bytes), tag byte 0x02 for Mutation::Delete (followed by 4-byte LE key length, key bytes), and tag byte 0x03 for a Commit entry (followed by the 32-byte root hash). IF a payload begins with an unknown tag byte, THE SYSTEM SHALL return WalError::ChecksumMismatch with a descriptive message — an unknown tag in an otherwise intact frame is data corruption, not truncation. THE SYSTEM SHALL NOT produce Mutation values with partial fields — if the payload length is inconsistent with the tag's required fields, this is a corrupted entry.
acceptance: A payload beginning with 0x01 and valid key/value fields deserialises to Mutation::Put { key, value }
A payload beginning with 0x02 and a valid key field deserialises to Mutation::Delete { key }
A payload beginning with 0x03 and 32 subsequent bytes deserialises to a Commit(Hash) variant
A payload beginning with an unknown tag (e.g. 0xFF) returns Err(WalError::ChecksumMismatch) with a message identifying the unknown tag
A Put payload where the declared key length exceeds the remaining payload bytes returns a corruption error, not a truncation sentinel

requirements.title: WalRecovery::recover — replay from last committed root forward
requirements.spec: WHEN WalRecovery::recover(initial_root) is called, THE SYSTEM SHALL scan the WAL file from the beginning, tracking the position of every Commit entry encountered. After locating the last Commit entry, THE SYSTEM SHALL replay all subsequent Put and Delete mutations into a new WalBuffer in the order they appear in the file. THE SYSTEM SHALL stop replay when it encounters a truncated entry (returning the WalBuffer as recovered so far — truncation is normal) or the end of file. THE SYSTEM SHALL return Err on encountering a corrupted entry (bad CRC32 or unknown tag) at any position. THE SYSTEM SHALL NOT replay mutations that precede the last Commit entry — those are already committed to the tree at initial_root. THE SYSTEM SHALL NOT modify the WAL file during recovery. IF the WAL file contains no Commit entries, THE SYSTEM SHALL replay all entries from the beginning, treating the provided initial_root as the baseline.
acceptance: Given a WAL with: Put(a=1), Commit(hash_A), Put(b=2), Put(c=3), recover() returns a WalBuffer containing exactly Put(b=2) and Put(c=3)
Given a WAL with: Put(a=1), Commit(hash_A), Put(b=2), [truncated frame], recover() returns Ok(WalBuffer{b=2}) — the truncated tail is accepted
Given a WAL with: Put(a=1), Commit(hash_A), [corrupt CRC32 frame], recover() returns Err(WalError::ChecksumMismatch)
Given a WAL with no Commit entries and: Put(a=1), Put(b=2), recover() returns a WalBuffer containing both mutations
Given a WAL with two Commit entries: Put(a=1), Commit(hash_A), Put(b=2), Commit(hash_B), Put(c=3), recover() returns a WalBuffer containing only Put(c=3)
The WAL file is unchanged (same byte length) after recover() completes

requirements.title: Wire WalRecovery into the wal module
requirements.spec: Add pub mod recovery to wal/mod.rs and re-export WalRecovery from the wal module so that the shard actor (CORE-007) can access it as haematite::wal::WalRecovery. Update crates/haematite/src/lib.rs to expose WalRecovery at the crate root if other public types from the wal module are already re-exported there. wal/mod.rs SHALL NOT contain any logic beyond pub mod declarations and pub use re-exports (CN3).
acceptance: crates/haematite/src/wal/mod.rs contains pub mod recovery and pub use recovery::WalRecovery
WalRecovery is accessible as haematite::wal::WalRecovery after the wiring
wal/mod.rs contains only pub mod and pub use statements — no struct definitions, functions, or trait implementations
cargo check -p haematite succeeds with WalRecovery wired in

boundaries: SHALL NOT implement the shard actor that calls WalRecovery::recover on restart — that is CORE-007
SHALL NOT truncate or compact the WAL file after recovery — WAL truncation is deferred to a future phase
SHALL NOT re-implement DurableWal's write path — recovery reads the format DurableWal writes; any format changes must be made in durable.rs and matched here
SHALL NOT replay mutations that are already committed to the prolly tree — only mutations after the last Commit entry are replayed
SHALL NOT perform any prolly tree operations — the returned WalBuffer is handed to the shard actor, which owns the tree
verification: cargo check -p haematite succeeds with no errors
cargo test -p haematite --lib passes all tests in the wal::recovery module
cargo clippy -p haematite -- -D warnings produces no warnings
grep -r 'unwrap()\|expect(' crates/haematite/src/wal/recovery.rs produces no matches outside #[cfg(test)] blocks
grep -n '.' crates/haematite/src/wal/mod.rs | grep -v 'pub mod\|pub use\|^[[:space:]]*$\|//' produces no output — mod.rs contains only declarations
A round-trip test: write N mutations and a commit to a DurableWal, then recover with WalRecovery and assert the returned WalBuffer contains exactly the post-commit mutations
